locations.name: Mogollon Mine
locations.description: The Mogollon Mine was organized in 1910 after the district's assayer confirmed gold values that made even the most skeptical investors reach for their checkbooks. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. A small town grew up around the mine: a post office, a two-room schoolhouse, three saloons, and the inevitable assay office that doubled as banker and source of local credit. Silicification of the wallrock created a hard, persistent hanging wall that minimized dilution and reduced the need for extensive timbering in the upper levels. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The stone assay office walls remain standing — thick enough to withstand a century of weather and neglect — while the surrounding wooden structures have long since collapsed. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.

locations.name: Last Chance Mine
locations.description: The Last Chance Mine dates to 1906, when surface copper carbonates — bright green and unmistakable against the gray country rock — led a prospecting party to stake claims on what proved to be a substantial porphyry deposit. The copper ore body was a classic porphyry-type deposit: large, low-grade, and requiring open-pit or large-scale underground block-caving methods to extract economically. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. Immigrant miners from Bohemia, Scotland, Wales, and Cornwall brought specific craft knowledge — the Welsh for their understanding of timbering, the Cornish for deep-shaft methods — that shaped the mine's technical character. The discovery of a second ore body, separated from the main vein by a barren porphyry dike, was made by an experienced shifter who noticed a change in wall-rock alteration that others had dismissed. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.

locations.name: Cooney Mine
locations.description: The Cooney Mine took shape in 1905 when a systematic sampling program confirmed that the silver deposit extended far enough at depth to justify a full underground development program. The silver occurred chiefly as argentite and native silver in a carbonate gangue, making hand-sorting practical and giving high-grade ore shipments an attractive economic profile. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. A Methodist church built by mine families in the camp's early years provided a counterpoint to the saloon culture, and its congregation was a force for stability in an otherwise transient population. A stock promotion scandal in the mine's middle years drove out the original investors and brought in new management, which proved to be an inadvertent improvement: the new superintendent was a better practical miner than his predecessor. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.

locations.name: Queen Mine
locations.description: Silver chloride ore lying loose on the surface drew the first prospectors to this ground, and in 1884 the Queen Mine was formally organized to systematically work the deposit. Bonanza-grade pockets — native silver in massive sheets — appeared periodically in the deepest workings, funding equipment upgrades and new shaft sinking for years. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.

locations.name: Silver Creek Mine
locations.description: Malachite and azurite staining on a hillside cliff band caught a geologist's eye in 1889, leading directly to the founding of the Silver Creek Mine and the opening of a new copper-mining chapter in the region. The secondary enrichment blanket near the water table carried chalcocite values several times higher than the primary ore below, providing a rich early phase of high-grade production. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. The company maintained a library of technical and general reading for its employees — an early example of the paternalistic amenities that larger mining corporations were beginning to offer in the reform era. A catastrophic underground fire in the upper stopes destroyed several hundred feet of timber sets and forced a three-month shutdown while the burned-out sections were rehabilitated. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.

locations.name: Fannie Mine
locations.description: The Fannie Mine was established in 1909 on a massive chalcopyrite body that local ranchers had been using as a landmark for years before anyone thought to test its assay value. The copper ore body was a classic porphyry-type deposit: large, low-grade, and requiring open-pit or large-scale underground block-caving methods to extract economically. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. The mine's payroll was the economic engine of the surrounding county, supporting farms, freighters, and merchants who collectively formed a community larger than the mine alone could explain. A granite porphyry intrusion had baked the surrounding sedimentary rocks into a resistant hornfels, and the skarn contact between the two formations proved to be the most productive ore zone in the district. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.

locations.name: Sandy Mine
locations.description: A chance glint of free gold in the tailings of an abandoned hydraulic pit led to the founding of the Sandy Mine in 1883, launching what would become one of the district's longer-lived operations. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. A mutual aid society formed by the miners provided modest death benefits and sick pay, filling a welfare gap that the company itself was slow to address. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.

locations.name: Maud S Mine
locations.description: The Maud S Mine opened in 1880 on copper-bearing skarn contact rocks that miners had to tunnel through cold limestone before reaching the ore zone proper. The chalcopyrite ore required concentration before smelting; the company erected a flotation mill that reduced the bulk of material shipped and dramatically lowered transport costs. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. Veterans of the Comstock, the California camps, and the Colorado silver fields found their way here, contributing deep practical knowledge to a management that was often long on capital and short on mining experience. The district's geology was more complex than the simple bonanza vein that early promoters had advertised; faults offset the ore body repeatedly, requiring careful surveying and systematic exploration to maintain continuity. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Today the old mine yard is quiet, the headframe long since dismantled for salvage, but the outline of the ore bins and the concrete footings of the hoist room are still legible to a practiced eye. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.

locations.name: Deep Well Mine
locations.description: In the second year of the big rush, 1870, the Deep Well Mine differentiated itself from dozens of flash-in-the-pan ventures by hitting a thick, continuous ore body at only forty feet of depth. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Today the old mine yard is quiet, the headframe long since dismantled for salvage, but the outline of the ore bins and the concrete footings of the hoist room are still legible to a practiced eye. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.

locations.name: Mogollon Chief Mine
locations.description: When oxidized silver minerals were found beneath a gossan cap in 1888, the Mogollon Chief Mine was organized and sinking was begun before the summer was out. Silver values were intimately associated with galena, and the concentrates commanded a favorable smelter schedule that rewarded careful ore preparation. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. The nearby town that developed to service the mine's workforce was a rough-edged but functional community, its main street lined with frame buildings that housed every service a working miner's paycheck could buy. A cave-in on the fourth level trapped fourteen men for thirty-six hours before rescuers broke through from an adjacent drift, an event that prompted the company to adopt a more rigorous ground-support standard. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The property is now managed as a recreational area; the underground workings are permanently sealed, but surface roads, tailings, and foundation outlines are accessible to visitors. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.

locations.name: Mogollon Star Lode
locations.description: An experienced drift miner who had spent fifteen years in the California camps identified the ore structure at this site in 1914 and organized the Mogollon Star Lode with a handful of trusted partners. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. Smallpox swept the camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and forcing the temporary suspension of production until the epidemic passed and the workforce could safely reassemble. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.

locations.name: Mogollon Belle
locations.description: Silver chloride ore lying loose on the surface drew the first prospectors to this ground, and in 1886 the Mogollon Belle was formally organized to systematically work the deposit. The silver occurred chiefly as argentite and native silver in a carbonate gangue, making hand-sorting practical and giving high-grade ore shipments an attractive economic profile. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. Women's contributions to the camp economy were substantial and often overlooked; they ran boarding houses, laundries, and small stores that collectively processed more money than the mine itself in some lean years. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The region's mining heritage tourism industry has found the Mogollon Belle site to be one of its most evocative stops, combining accessible ruins with a well-documented historical record. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.

locations.name: Mogollon Eagle
locations.description: The Mogollon Eagle was established in 1882 on a massive chalcopyrite body that local ranchers had been using as a landmark for years before anyone thought to test its assay value. Native copper appeared sporadically in the upper workings — not enough to transform the economics, but enough to sustain early investor enthusiasm through the lean development years. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. The deposit was zoned: near-surface gold grading into silver-dominant ore at depth, with lead-zinc values increasing in the lowest levels — a telescoped epithermal system that geologists found instructive. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The site is accessible via a graded dirt road maintained by the county, and the surviving structures are visible from the road without the need to enter the fenced hazard zone around the shaft collar. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.

locations.name: Mogollon Pride
locations.description: A district already buzzing with silver excitement welcomed the Mogollon Pride in 1879; the new property quickly proved that the ore body extended well beyond the surface outcrop. The complex mineralogy — argentite, polybasite, and stephanite alongside common galena — required a selective milling approach that the company's metallurgist took months to perfect. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.

locations.name: Mogollon Consolidated
locations.description: The Mogollon Consolidated opened its first level in 1880 on a carbonate silver deposit that the local assayer valued at extraordinary grades — grades that drove frantic development through the next decade. Silver values were intimately associated with galena, and the concentrates commanded a favorable smelter schedule that rewarded careful ore preparation. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. Fire destroyed the mill building in the mine's eleventh year; rather than rebuild the stamp mill, the company erected a modern concentrating plant that improved metal recovery significantly. The mine's scale placed it in a different category from its neighbors: where most operations were personal enterprises, this was a corporate undertaking with professional engineers, a company hospital, and its own railroad siding. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.

locations.name: Mogollon Fraction
locations.description: Silver-lead float scattered across the hillside in 1888 told experienced prospectors exactly where to sink the first shaft of the Mogollon Fraction. The silver occurred chiefly as argentite and native silver in a carbonate gangue, making hand-sorting practical and giving high-grade ore shipments an attractive economic profile. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. Women's contributions to the camp economy were substantial and often overlooked; they ran boarding houses, laundries, and small stores that collectively processed more money than the mine itself in some lean years. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. The site is accessible via a graded dirt road maintained by the county, and the surviving structures are visible from the road without the need to enter the fenced hazard zone around the shaft collar. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.

locations.name: Whitewater Creek Mine
locations.description: The Whitewater Creek Mine came into being in 1914 when assays of dark galena boulders revealed silver values that convinced a cautious mining company to commit full capital to the district. Assay grades were erratic, reflecting the pocket nature of silver-bearing ore in this district; a week of bonanza followed a month of development work on barren vein matter. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. A two-room schoolhouse built by the mining company's wives' auxiliary educated the district's children through all twelve grades at its peak enrollment. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. A state historic marker at the road junction explains the mine's significance; visitors who venture up the old access road find the ruins more extensive than the marker suggests. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.

locations.name: Gilita Creek Mine
locations.description: In 1897, investors drawn by reports of bonanza silver ore in the district backed the founding of the Gilita Creek Mine, expecting quick returns and getting a more complex ore body than they bargained for. The silver occurred chiefly as argentite and native silver in a carbonate gangue, making hand-sorting practical and giving high-grade ore shipments an attractive economic profile. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. The mine employed a physician during peak operations, a relatively unusual luxury that spoke to the company's interest in keeping its skilled workforce healthy and on-site. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The site has been partially reclaimed; the tailings were graded and seeded, the shaft capped with concrete, and the most hazardous open workings fenced — though the outline of the camp is still unmistakable from above. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.

locations.name: Mogollon Placer No.1
locations.description: A railroad-construction geologist filed a confidential report in 1875 that described the copper deposit that would become the Mogollon Placer No.1; private capital moved quickly to acquire the ground. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.

locations.name: Mogollon Placer No.2
locations.description: The Mogollon Placer No.2 was incorporated in 1906 on ground that Indian and Mexican miners had worked by hand for years; the new company brought steam power and ambition to what had been a subsistence operation. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.

locations.name: Mogollon Silver Lode
locations.description: Copper staining and leached capping had been noted by travelers through this canyon for a generation before a properly financed company organized the Mogollon Silver Lode in 1913. locations.name: Mogollon Copper Lode
locations.description: The Mogollon Copper Lode took shape in 1881 when a systematic sampling program confirmed that the silver deposit extended far enough at depth to justify a full underground development program. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.

locations.name: Mogollon Gold Lode
locations.description: The Mogollon Gold Lode took shape in 1887 when a systematic sampling program confirmed that the silver deposit extended far enough at depth to justify a full underground development program. The ore ran high in silver with notable lead credits, and the smelter's payment schedule for lead kept the mine solvent during periods when silver prices weakened. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. A small town grew up around the mine: a post office, a two-room schoolhouse, three saloons, and the inevitable assay office that doubled as banker and source of local credit. The ore body was controlled by a northwest-trending fault that focused hydrothermal fluids against an impermeable diorite dike — classic structural geology that the mine's long-time geologist had predicted before the drill proved it. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The site is accessible via a graded dirt road maintained by the county, and the surviving structures are visible from the road without the need to enter the fenced hazard zone around the shaft collar. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.

locations.name: Glenwood Mine
locations.description: The Glenwood Mine was among the first deep mines in the district, sinking past the 1901 surface placers to reach the primary lode that would prove its real value. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. Wages at this mine tracked the district average closely, and the company avoided some of the worst labor conflicts of the era by maintaining steady employment through the inevitable ore-grade fluctuations. A stock promotion scandal in the mine's middle years drove out the original investors and brought in new management, which proved to be an inadvertent improvement: the new superintendent was a better practical miner than his predecessor. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.

locations.name: Homestake Mine (NM)
locations.description: Gold fever brought Homestake Mine (NM) to life in 1905, when prospectors following a free-gold float trace up a creek drainage broke into a promising quartz vein and staked the first claims. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. Fire destroyed the mill building in the mine's eleventh year; rather than rebuild the stamp mill, the company erected a modern concentrating plant that improved metal recovery significantly. At its zenith the mine employed over three hundred men on three shifts, running a powerful hoisting plant and a large concentration mill that operated without interruption for months at a stretch. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.
locations.notes: White Oaks district, Lincoln County, NM

locations.name: Old Abe Mine
locations.description: The Old Abe Mine was among the first deep mines in the district, sinking past the 1889 surface placers to reach the primary lode that would prove its real value. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. The mine employed a physician during peak operations, a relatively unusual luxury that spoke to the company's interest in keeping its skilled workforce healthy and on-site. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Surface structures were demolished for salvage within a decade of closure, leaving only the tailings, a few stone walls, and the collapsed collar of the main shaft to mark the location. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.
locations.notes: White Oaks district, Lincoln County, NM

locations.name: South Homestake Mine
locations.description: The South Homestake Mine was organized in 1897 after the district's assayer confirmed gold values that made even the most skeptical investors reach for their checkbooks. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. The mine's payroll was the economic engine of the surrounding county, supporting farms, freighters, and merchants who collectively formed a community larger than the mine alone could explain. Silicification of the wallrock created a hard, persistent hanging wall that minimized dilution and reduced the need for extensive timbering in the upper levels. The mine's scale placed it in a different category from its neighbors: where most operations were personal enterprises, this was a corporate undertaking with professional engineers, a company hospital, and its own railroad siding. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.
locations.notes: White Oaks district, Lincoln County, NM

locations.name: White Oaks Mine
locations.description: Cornish miners who had drifted north from the California camps staked the White Oaks Mine in 1881, bringing their tradition of deep, systematic hard-rock work to what had been a surface-only district. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. Family men who planned to stay rather than drift formed the backbone of the camp's permanent population, building small farms on nearby land and sending their children to the district school. The discovery of a second ore body, separated from the main vein by a barren porphyry dike, was made by an experienced shifter who noticed a change in wall-rock alteration that others had dismissed. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.
locations.notes: White Oaks district, Lincoln County, NM

locations.name: North Homestake Mine
locations.description: A party of Welsh hard-rock miners who arrived in 1899 recognized the geology at once — the pyritized schist, the carbonate alteration — and staked the North Homestake Mine before the week was out. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. Tent cities gave way to frame structures within the first year, and within three years the camp had taken on the permanence of a real town, complete with a debating society and a brass band. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. The operation ranked among the district's largest, with a workforce exceeding two hundred in peak years and a mill complex capable of processing hundreds of tons of ore per day. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.
locations.notes: White Oaks district, Lincoln County, NM

locations.name: American Mine
locations.description: In the second year of the big rush, 1879, the American Mine differentiated itself from dozens of flash-in-the-pan ventures by hitting a thick, continuous ore body at only forty feet of depth. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. The discovery of a second ore body, separated from the main vein by a barren porphyry dike, was made by an experienced shifter who noticed a change in wall-rock alteration that others had dismissed. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Much of this district falls under BLM jurisdiction where recreational metal detecting is permitted; check the land status map for the specific parcel before visiting.
locations.notes: White Oaks district, Lincoln County, NM

locations.name: Baxter Mine
locations.description: In the second year of the big rush, 1905, the Baxter Mine differentiated itself from dozens of flash-in-the-pan ventures by hitting a thick, continuous ore body at only forty feet of depth. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. A polyglot workforce gave the camp its distinctive character: Serbian and Croatian miners who had come from the copper mines of Central Europe occupied one end of camp, while Mexican miners who had followed silver veins north formed a tight-knit community at the other. The ore body was controlled by a northwest-trending fault that focused hydrothermal fluids against an impermeable diorite dike — classic structural geology that the mine's long-time geologist had predicted before the drill proved it. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. The surface plant has mostly vanished, but the mine's legacy persists in county records, family histories, and the occasional specimen jar of ore that turns up at a local antique fair. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.

locations.name: Little Mac Mine
locations.description: In the second year of the big rush, 1910, the Little Mac Mine differentiated itself from dozens of flash-in-the-pan ventures by hitting a thick, continuous ore body at only forty feet of depth. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. Fourth of July celebrations at the mine drew miners from a fifty-mile radius, the day marked by drilling competitions, mule races, and the communal roasting of beef over wood fires. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.
locations.notes: White Oaks district, Lincoln County, NM

locations.name: Lone Star Mine
locations.description: Brothers who had worked the Comstock bonanzas for a decade brought their savings and their skills to this district in 1892 and founded the Lone Star Mine on a vein they traced across three separate outcroppings. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. The workforce was drawn from across the mining West: Cornish 'Cousin Jacks' ran the drilling crews, Irish muckers kept the stopes clean, Scandinavians handled the timber work, and Chinese laborers managed the most dangerous surface jobs. The ore body was controlled by a northwest-trending fault that focused hydrothermal fluids against an impermeable diorite dike — classic structural geology that the mine's long-time geologist had predicted before the drill proved it. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Surface structures were demolished for salvage within a decade of closure, leaving only the tailings, a few stone walls, and the collapsed collar of the main shaft to mark the location. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.
locations.notes: White Oaks district, Lincoln County, NM

locations.name: Lucky Mine
locations.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1897 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Lucky Mine and begun sinking. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. The mine employed a physician during peak operations, a relatively unusual luxury that spoke to the company's interest in keeping its skilled workforce healthy and on-site. Smallpox swept the camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and forcing the temporary suspension of production until the epidemic passed and the workforce could safely reassemble. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.
locations.notes: White Oaks district, Lincoln County, NM

locations.name: White Oaks Placer No.1
locations.description: The White Oaks Placer No.1 was incorporated in 1886 following a season of intensive surface sampling that convinced its organizers the deposit was large enough to justify a major capital investment. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. Fourth of July celebrations at the mine drew miners from a fifty-mile radius, the day marked by drilling competitions, mule races, and the communal roasting of beef over wood fires. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.
locations.notes: White Oaks district, Lincoln County, NM

locations.name: White Oaks Placer No.2
locations.description: The White Oaks Placer No.2 dates to 1894, a period when the rush was still fresh and optimism ran high enough to justify the heavy capitalization needed to build a proper stamp mill. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. Fourth of July celebrations at the mine drew miners from a fifty-mile radius, the day marked by drilling competitions, mule races, and the communal roasting of beef over wood fires. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. A local historical society has stabilized the surviving headframe and ore bins, and occasional guided tours bring a trickle of visitors who connect the industrial ruin to the period photographs on display inside. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.
locations.notes: White Oaks district, Lincoln County, NM

locations.name: White Oaks Star Lode
locations.description: The White Oaks Star Lode began as a placer strike in 1879: a forty-niner scooping creek gravel noticed colors rich enough to warrant hard-rock follow-up, and within months a shaft was sinking. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. A two-room schoolhouse built by the mining company's wives' auxiliary educated the district's children through all twelve grades at its peak enrollment. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.
locations.notes: White Oaks district, Lincoln County, NM

locations.name: White Oaks Belle
locations.description: The White Oaks Belle was organized in 1886 after the district's assayer confirmed gold values that made even the most skeptical investors reach for their checkbooks. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. The deposit was zoned: near-surface gold grading into silver-dominant ore at depth, with lead-zinc values increasing in the lowest levels — a telescoped epithermal system that geologists found instructive. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.

locations.description: Cornish miners who had drifted north from the California camps staked the White Oaks Eagle in 1888, bringing their tradition of deep, systematic hard-rock work to what had been a surface-only district. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The property is now managed as a recreational area; the underground workings are permanently sealed, but surface roads, tailings, and foundation outlines are accessible to visitors. Metal detecting is permitted on the BLM sections of this property; the New Mexico State Land Office manages adjacent parcels under different rules, so confirm the boundary before detecting.

locations.name: White Oaks Pride
locations.description: The White Oaks Pride traces its origins to 1909, when a prospector whose horse stumbled on the hillside noticed the exposed quartz float and recognized the classic markers of a primary gold deposit. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. Veterans of the Comstock, the California camps, and the Colorado silver fields found their way here, contributing deep practical knowledge to a management that was often long on capital and short on mining experience. An unusually rich ore chimney was discovered when development drifts on two adjacent levels connected unexpectedly through the ore body itself, revealing a vertical pipe of native metal that astonished even veterans. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Today the old mine yard is quiet, the headframe long since dismantled for salvage, but the outline of the ore bins and the concrete footings of the hoist room are still legible to a practiced eye. Metal detecting is generally allowed on the BLM-administered portions of this New Mexico district under casual-use provisions — verify the parcel boundary before heading out.

locations.name: White Oaks Consolidated
locations.description: The White Oaks Consolidated was incorporated in 1903 on ground that Indian and Mexican miners had worked by hand for years; the new company brought steam power and ambition to what had been a subsistence operation. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. Immigrant miners from Bohemia, Scotland, Wales, and Cornwall brought specific craft knowledge — the Welsh for their understanding of timbering, the Cornish for deep-shaft methods — that shaped the mine's technical character. A runaway ore train on the inclined haulage wrecked the ore bin and damaged the crusher, shutting down production for three weeks while repairs were made and the haulage brake system redesigned. The operation ranked among the district's largest, with a workforce exceeding two hundred in peak years and a mill complex capable of processing hundreds of tons of ore per day. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. locations.name: Nogal Mine
locations.description: The Nogal Mine opened in 1896 after a grizzled veteran spotted the telltale iron-stained gossan capping a rich lode — the kind of surface sign that experienced eyes never miss. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it.
